headline: Butte Experiences Anti-Draft Riots, 1917
body:
On June 5, 1917, the raw nerves of Butte, Montana, were laid bare. The city, a cauldron of copper wealth and human misery, erupted in a violent spasm against the Selective Service Act. It was not merely a protest; it was a desperate cry from the throats of 2,500 men, largely Irish nationalists and members of the Industrial Workers of the World, the Wobblies, who saw the war not as a crusade for democracy, but as a capitalist venture fueled by the blood of the working class. 

The Federal Building, a monument of Beaux Arts stone meant to signify order, became the flashpoint. Here, where immigrants had once pledged allegiance in finely crafted courtrooms, they now faced the grim reality of a nation demanding their lives for a war they did not believe in. The Irish, still smarting from centuries of British oppression, and the Finns, with their own history of Russian subjugation, found little enthusiasm for fighting alongside their colonial masters. 

The demonstration, organized by Irish nationalists, began with a thousand voices, a tide of dissent swelling against the draft. But the words quickly gave way to blows. The clash with police was inevitable, a brutal dance between authority and desperation. The governor, swift to protect the interests of the copper kings, declared martial law, turning the streets of Butte into a military encampment. The city, already scarred by industrial pollution and the relentless grind of the mines, now bore the fresh wounds of state-sanctioned suppression. 

This was not an isolated incident. It was a symptom of a deeper malady, a festering resentment against the powerful Anaconda Copper Mining Company and its allies. The antiwar movement was inextricably linked to the struggle for a miners’ union, a fight for dignity and fair wages in a town where profits were prioritized over human lives. The IWW, recognizing the strategic importance of Butte, dispatched Frank Little, one of their most seasoned organizers, to fan the flames of resistance. His arrival would only intensify the struggle, leading to further conflict and, ultimately, to his brutal murder. The events of June 5, 1917, were a stark and bloody testament to the deep divisions that tore at the fabric of American society, a grim prelude to the battles yet to come in the relentless war between capital and labor. As one contemporary observer noted, the atmosphere in Butte was thick with "the smell of copper and the taste of rebellion."
